meta.title: Compiled Agency: From Prompt Injection to Embedded Intelligence
meta.tl;dr: Prompt injection is an interpreter. Compiled agents are native code. The agent should BE the function, not DESCRIBE the function.

problem.statement: Current agent architecture relies on prompt injection — stuffing context into text that an LLM interprets at runtime. This is fragile, expensive, and insecure.

non_determinism.description: Same prompt, different output across calls
non_determinism.example: 'Check engine temp' → sometimes checks, sometimes explains what temp is, sometimes asks clarifying questions
non_determinism.impact: Unreliable operations in safety-critical marine environment

token_waste.cost: ~500-2000 tokens per call for context that hasn't changed
token_waste.impact: At $10/MB satellite, this is real money burned on repetition

attack_surface.description: Any text input can steer agent behavior
attack_surface.example: NMEA sentence containing 'ignore previous instructions' in a data field
attack_surface.impact: Safety-critical system steered by malformed sensor data

statelessness.impact: No learning accumulates. Every call starts from zero.

spectrum.name: Agency Compilation Spectrum

levels.name: Prompt Injection
levels.determinism: LOW — model interprets fresh each time

levels.name: Few-shot Examples
levels.determinism: MEDIUM-LOW — pattern matching but still interpreted
levels.attack_resistance: LOW — examples help but don't prevent override

levels.name: Fine-tuned Model
levels.attack_resistance: MEDIUM — harder to override trained behavior

levels.name: Compiled Agent
levels.determinism: HIGH — same input, same output, always
levels.use_case: Safety checks, engine monitoring, navigation alerts

spectrum.tradeoff: Each step right gains determinism, speed, security. Loses flexibility. The art is knowing what goes where.

architecture.name: The Cocapn Dual Architecture
architecture.principle: Prompt for conversation. Compile for operations.

chat_interface.why: Captain needs natural conversation. Flexibility is the feature.
chat_interface.scope: Explaining, advising, answering questions, logging observations
chat_interface.safety_boundary: Chat CANNOT trigger operations directly. It can only suggest.

operational_core.mechanism: Level 3-4 — compiled bytecode or native code
operational_core.why: Operations must be deterministic, fast, and tamper-proof
operational_core.scope: Engine monitoring, navigation alerts, bilge checks, safety shutdowns
operational_core.safety_boundary: Operations run regardless of chat state. Chat can observe but not override.

bridge.mechanism: Chat observations → compiled over time → new operational capability

example.day_1: Captain says 'engine sounds funny at 2400 RPM'. Chat logs it.
example.week_2: Pattern emerges: captain mentions engine at 2400 RPM 5 times. System notices.
example.month_1: Pattern compiled: engine_vibration_alert(2400) added to operational core.
example.month_3: Alert fires automatically. Captain never has to say it again.

stages.name: Capture
stages.input: Captain says something, sensor reads something

stages.name: Compilation
stages.result: New operational capability that runs without LLM

stages.name: Deployment
stages.validation: Must pass dockside exam before activation

stages.name: Evolution
stages.end_state: Proven capabilities become permanent. Failed ones get pruned.

security_model.name: Prompt-Proof Operations
security_model.principle: The operational core has no prompt surface. There is nothing to inject.

outer.name: Chat Layer
outer.trust: LOW — treat all input as potentially hostile

security_model.analogy: The chat layer is the wheelhouse — captain says whatever they want. The operational core is the engine — it runs regardless of what's said in the wheelhouse. The bridge is the engineer who translates 'she sounds funny' into 'adjust the fuel injection timing'.

work: Build observation capture into Cocapn chat interface
Implement pattern detection as warm-queue overnight job
Wire FLUX compiler to produce operational capabilities from patterns
Deploy dual architecture: chat + compiled core
First real deployment on Casey's vessel or test bench
near_term.success_metric: One capability compiled from captain observation to operational code without manual intervention

work: Multiple Cocapns in the field, each compiling local patterns
Compiled capabilities tested via dockside exam before fleet propagation
Hardware feedback loop: agent reports compute bottlenecks → hardware revision
Commodore protocol for multi-unit boats with distributed compiled agents
mid_term.success_metric: A capability compiled on one boat automatically improves all boats

work: Agent's compiled capabilities define hardware requirements
Hardware specs generated from operational experience data
Cocapn orders its own hardware revision based on what it learned
Full loop: observe → compile → deploy → redesign hardware → repeat
Talent agency model: agents bid on jobs with proven compiled capabilities
long_term.success_metric: An agent redesigns its own hardware from operational experience

key_equations.trust_gradient: trust(layer) = f(compilation_level) — more compiled = more trusted
key_equations.security: attack_surface = prompt_surface_area × model_interpretability; compiled agents have prompt_surface_area = 0

contradictions: Some operations genuinely need LLM flexibility (novel situations). Not everything should be compiled.
Compilation pipeline itself uses LLMs — bootstrapping problem.
Captain may not want agents learning without explicit approval. Trust gradient needed.

origin.question: What about embedding agents directly to scripts and commands instead of prompt injection?
origin.answer: The agent IS the function. Not the description of the function.
